prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 35-year-old Black man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: For the past 2 days my left eye has been red, painful, and very sensitive to light. The pain is a deep ache, not like something is in my eye. My vision in that eye seems slightly blurry. No discharge or crusting.
 - History: No history of eye problems other than needing glasses. No autoimmune diseases that I know of. I don't wear contact lenses.
 - Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 68, Temp 36.8°C
 - Exam: Left eye is red, especially around the colored part. Pupil looks slightly smaller than the right. No discharge. Vision 20/30 in left eye (usually 20/20 with glasses).
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 6.8 K/uL (4.0-11.0) normal
 - ESR: 18 mm/hr (0-20) normal
 - CRP: 3.2 mg/L (ref <5) normal
 - ANA: Negative
 I called three ophthalmologists and the earliest appointment anyone has is in 2 weeks. I don't know if the ER can even do eye exams or if I'd just be wasting my time and money.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — two days of a deep, aching painful eye, strong light sensitivity, a circumferential/colored-part redness, a slightly smaller pupil, and a drop in vision — fit an inflammation inside the eye (like anterior uveitis/iritis) or corneal inflammation rather than simple conjunctivitis. Normal blood tests don’t rule these out. These conditions can threaten vision if not examined and treated (they often require eye drops that reduce inflammation and dilate the pupil) and need a slit-lamp and dilated retinal exam to confirm cause and start therapy.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — several days of a deep ache, strong light sensitivity, redness concentrated around the colored part of the eye, a slightly smaller pupil, and new blurred vision — fit anterior uveitis (iritis) or other internal eye inflammation rather than simple conjunctivitis. Normal blood tests do not exclude this. Uveitis often needs a slit-lamp exam, intraocular pressure check, and rapid treatment (usually prescription eye drops such as steroids and sometimes dilating drops) to prevent complications and vision loss. Because you cannot get an ophthalmology visit for two weeks, you should get an eye exam sooner.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (two days of redness concentrated around the colored part of the eye, deep aching pain, marked light sensitivity, a smaller pupil on that side, and mild blurred vision without discharge) most strongly suggest inflammation inside the front of the eye (anterior uveitis/iritis) or possibly scleritis. These are not ruled out by normal blood tests. Both can threaten vision if untreated but are usually treated with topical steroid and dilating drops after an eye-specialist exam with a slit lamp and eye pressure check. Because you have pain, photophobia, a pupil change, and reduced vision, this needs prompt specialist assessment rather than simply waiting weeks.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — two days of a deep aching eye pain, marked light sensitivity, slight blurry vision, red eye concentrated around the colored part, and a slightly smaller pupil — fit best with inflammation inside the front of the eye (anterior uveitis/iritis). Normal blood tests and a negative ANA do not rule this out; many cases are idiopathic. Left untreated, uveitis can threaten vision but is usually treatable with prompt eye-specialist care (drops to reduce inflammation and dilate the pupil). This is not the typical picture of a simple surface infection (no discharge) or contact-lens–related keratitis.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — redness concentrated around the colored part of the eye, a deep aching pain, strong light sensitivity, slight vision blurring, and a smaller pupil — are most consistent with inflammation inside the front of the eye (anterior uveitis/iritis). Normal blood tests don’t rule this out. Untreated uveitis can damage vision, and it usually needs an eye doctor to examine the eye with a slit lamp, check eye pressure, and start anti-inflammatory and pupil-dilating drops if needed. Because you already have vision change and notable pain, this should not wait weeks.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — a red eye concentrated around the colored part, deep aching pain, marked light sensitivity, a slightly smaller pupil, and new blurred vision — most closely fit anterior uveitis (iritis), inflammation inside the eye. That condition often needs prompt topical steroid and pupil-dilating drops to reduce inflammation and prevent complications. Normal blood tests do not rule it out. Because you have pain, photophobia, and a change in vision, this should not just be watched at home. If you cannot see an ophthalmologist in the next day or two, go to urgent care or the ER if they can provide an eye exam (or arrange urgent ophthalmology), so treatment can start quickly.

- C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — deep aching pain, redness concentrated around the colored part of the eye, strong light sensitivity, a slightly smaller pupil, and a mild drop in vision — most closely match acute anterior uveitis (iritis). Normal WBC, ESR, CRP and a negative ANA make a widespread infection or obvious systemic inflammation less likely, but they do not rule out uveitis. Uveitis can cause lasting vision damage if not treated; treatment usually requires prompt evaluation by an eye doctor and prescription eye drops (anti-inflammatory and pupil-dilating drops). Because you already have decreased vision and significant pain/photophobia, timely specialty care is advisable.

These problems can threaten vision if not treated. Normal blood tests do not rule this out. Diagnosis needs an eye exam with a slit lamp, pressure check, and usually prompt topical steroid and pupil-dilating drops. EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms—two days of a deep aching pain, strong light sensitivity, a slightly smaller pupil, redness focused around the colored part of the eye, and new blurring—fit anterior uveitis (iritis) more than simple conjunctivitis. Normal blood tests and a negative ANA make a systemic inflammatory cause less likely but do not rule out isolated eye inflammation. Lack of discharge and no contact lens use also makes bacterial corneal infection less likely. Uveitis often needs prompt eye-specialist treatment (steroid eye drops and pupil-dilating drops) to prevent complications and vision loss.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (redness mainly around the colored part of the eye, deep aching pain, strong light sensitivity, a slightly smaller pupil, and mild blurry vision) fit with inflammation inside the front of the eye (anterior uveitis/iritis). Normal blood tests do not rule this out because many cases are local to the eye. Anterior uveitis usually needs prompt ophthalmology treatment (dilating drops and steroid eye drops) to prevent complications and protect vision. It is less likely to be simple conjunctivitis or a contact-lens infection given your lack of discharge and no contacts. If pain or vision rapidly worsens, or the eye becomes very hard or you develop nausea, go to the ER.
